top_strategic.title: Sell the Idea Lab itself
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Every domain could benefit from an AI research team. Package it.

multi_model_check.change_mind: Evidence is a market observation, not proof of feasibility. Change my mind with: (a) comparable successful lab-as-a-service examples with unit economics, (b) customer discovery showing willingness to pay premium for packaged research vs. hiring own researchers, (c) differentiation strategy—why this lab's IP is defensible across domains.
multi_model_check.blind_spot: Assumes domains are interchangeable; ignores domain-specific research maturity. Pharmaceutical research labs won't buy generic 'AI research' the same way SaaS startups might. No segmentation by willingness/ability to pay or research complexity.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Next step to validate 'Sell the Idea Lab itself'?

top_strategic.title: Crowdsource the corpus
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Incentivize users to contribute data in exchange for insights.

multi_model_check.change_mind: (a) Proof users will contribute *quality* data at scale without free-riding; (b) mechanism showing insight-sharing doesn't commoditize your own research moat; (c) case study of crowdsourced corpus that stayed proprietary AND valuable; (d) incentive structure preventing adverse selection (garbage data submissions).
multi_model_check.blind_spot: The 'insights' offered as incentive are vague—if valuable enough to motivate contribution, are they valuable enough to keep proprietary? Ignores reputation/gaming attacks in crowdsourced systems. No analysis of data quality decay over time or privacy implications of user contributions.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Next step to validate 'Crowdsource the corpus'?

top_strategic.title: Invert the business model
top_strategic.evidence_summary: What if the data subjects became the customers?

multi_model_check.change_mind: (a) Specific examples of data subjects successfully monetizing (not just being surveyed); (b) willingness-to-pay research showing subjects value insights enough to sustain unit economics; (c) resolution of the consent/privacy paradox—if data subjects are customers, what are they buying vs. selling?; (d) comparison to failed platforms that attempted this (most have).
multi_model_check.blind_spot: Conflates data subjects with customers—these are opposite poles in most business models for good reason. No acknowledgment that the most valuable data subjects (wealthy, data-rich individuals) are least incentivized, while least valuable (data-poor) are most interested. Ignores regulatory friction when you flip from 'data processor' to 'data purchaser' legally.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Next step to validate 'Invert the business model'?

top_strategic.title: API-first distribution
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Make findings consumable by other apps via public API.

multi_model_check.change_mind: (a) User research showing API consumption demand (not just supply-side assumption); (b) competitive analysis of API monetization strategies in research/data space—what works?; (c) cost model for API maintenance, versioning, support; (d) examples where open APIs didn't cannibalize direct revenue.
multi_model_check.blind_spot: Distribution ≠ monetization. Open APIs often commoditize findings and reduce pricing power. No mention of support burden, version fragmentation, or free-tier abuse. Assumes 'consumability' is the bottleneck—usually it's integrating proprietary research into existing workflows.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Next step to validate 'API-first distribution'?

top_strategic.title: Partner with adjacent platforms
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Cross-promote with platforms that have complementary data.

multi_model_check.change_mind: (a) Specific platforms identified with *demonstrated* complementary data and aligned incentives; (b) success metrics defining what 'adjacent' means (overlap in customer, data, or use case?); (c) worked example of cross-promotion generating measurable ROI; (d) honesty about whether you're a junior partner or equal in these deals.
multi_model_check.blind_spot: Most vague finding. 'Adjacent' is undefined—overlapping customers aren't complementary data. Ignores negotiating power imbalance (larger platforms set terms). No discussion of channel conflict: if partner platform offers similar insights, you're competing. Assumes goodwill partnerships without addressing incentive misalignment.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Next step to validate 'Partner with adjacent platforms'?

top_data.title: Strong positive correlation: skills.consistency ↔ skills.composure (r=0.90)
top_data.evidence_summary: Across 4312 records, 'skills.consistency' and 'skills.composure' show a strong positive correlation (Pearson r = 0.901). This may indicate a causal relationship or shared underlying factor.
top_data.recommended_action: Investigate the mechanism behind the skills.consistency↔skills.composure relationship. Can one predict the other? Would an intervention on one change the other?

follow_up.follow_up_question: Next step to validate 'Strong positive correlation: skills.consistency ↔ '?
follow_up.proposed_action: Investigate the mechanism behind the skills.consistency↔skills.composure relationship. Can one predict the other? Would an intervention on one change the other?

top_data.title: 'arc' dominated by 'strategic' (84% of values)
top_data.evidence_summary: In field 'arc', the value 'strategic' appears 3603 times out of 4313 total observations (83.5%). Second-place 'grind_rally' at only 4.4%. This dominance may mask other signals.
top_data.recommended_action: Create balanced samples that equalize 'arc' values. Current analyses are biased toward 'strategic'.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Next step to validate ''arc' dominated by 'strategic' (84% of values)'?
follow_up.proposed_action: Create balanced samples that equalize 'arc' values. Current analyses are biased toward 'strategic'.

top_data.title: Archetype: 'Low-Quality Casual Content' (1181 records, 27% of corpus)
top_data.evidence_summary: Found 1181 records matching this archetype (27.4% of corpus). Feature profile: {'quality': 'L', 'viral': 'L', 'watchability': 'L', 'total_shots': 'L', 'skills.court_coverage': 'L', 'skills.kitchen': 'L'}. This is a distinct segment worth analyzing separately.
top_data.recommended_action: Create a targeted analysis or product for the 'Low-Quality Casual Content' segment. What do these records have in common beyond the feature profile?

source_records.summary: A prolonged kitchen dink rally ends with an unforced error into the net.
A pickleball serve hits the net and fails to cross, ending the point.
A pickleball rally features consistent dinks at the net after a missed serve.

follow_up.follow_up_question: Next step to validate 'Archetype: 'Low-Quality Casual Content' (1181 reco'?
follow_up.proposed_action: Create a targeted analysis or product for the 'Low-Quality Casual Content' segment. What do these records have in common beyond the feature profile?

top_data.title: 'quality' has 161 strong outliers (|z|>2.5, mean=6.18, std=0.77)
top_data.evidence_summary: Field 'quality' has mean 6.18 and stdev 0.77 across 4313 records. 161 records are more than 2.5 standard deviations away. This is 3.7% of the corpus — enough to investigate but not so much it's noise.
top_data.recommended_action: Investigate what makes 'quality' outliers different. Are they data quality issues or genuinely interesting cases?

source_records.summary: Players engage in a prolonged kitchen dink rally, demonstrating patience and control.

follow_up.proposed_action: Investigate what makes 'quality' outliers different. Are they data quality issues or genuinely interesting cases?

top_data.title: 'arc' has 7 rare values (2-5 appearances)
top_data.evidence_summary: Field 'arc' has a long tail: 7 distinct values appear only 2-5 times. Examples: strategic_play, comedy, athletic_rally, athletic_highlight, clutch_moment. These could be emerging trends or noise.
top_data.recommended_action: Monitor rare 'arc' values over time — growing ones are emerging trends.
